provisions.title: Strengthening Financial Regulation: Origins
provisions.text: At the Washington Summit in November 2008, G20 leaders committed to a comprehensive reform of the global financial system in response to the global financial crisis. Key commitments included: strengthening transparency and accountability; enhancing sound regulation; promoting integrity in financial markets; reinforcing international cooperation; and reforming international financial institutions. The Washington declaration established the framework for the post-crisis regulatory reform agenda.

provisions.title: Establishing the FSB
provisions.text: At the London Summit in April 2009, G20 leaders agreed to establish the Financial Stability Board (FSB) as the successor to the Financial Stability Forum (FSF), with a broadened mandate to promote financial stability. The FSB was tasked with: assessing vulnerabilities affecting the financial system; promoting coordination among authorities responsible for financial stability; monitoring and advising on market developments; assessing best practice in meeting regulatory standards; undertaking joint strategic reviews of standard-setting bodies; and setting guidelines for supervisory colleges.

provisions.title: Basel III Endorsement
provisions.text: At the Seoul Summit in November 2010, G20 leaders endorsed the Basel III framework for bank capital and liquidity. The endorsement covered: higher minimum capital requirements (CET1 4.5%, Tier 1 6%, total capital 8%); capital conservation buffer (2.5%); countercyclical buffer (0-2.5%); the Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR); and the Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR). Leaders committed to consistent implementation across jurisdictions according to the agreed transition schedule.

provisions.title: Tax Transparency and BEPS
provisions.text: At the St Petersburg Summit in 2013, G20 leaders endorsed the OECD/G20 Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (BEPS) Action Plan to address tax avoidance by multinational enterprises. Although primarily a tax initiative, the BEPS project intersects with financial regulation through: transparency of beneficial ownership; automatic exchange of financial account information (Common Reporting Standard); country-by-country reporting by multinational enterprises; and addressing hybrid mismatch arrangements in the financial sector.

provisions.title: Financial Innovation and Regulatory Response
provisions.text: At the Osaka Summit in 2019, G20 leaders welcomed the FSB's work on the financial stability implications of technological innovation. Key areas addressed include: BigTech in finance (regulatory implications of large technology firms entering financial services); decentralised financial technologies (distributed ledger, smart contracts); artificial intelligence and machine learning in financial services; and cloud computing (concentration risks). Leaders called for continued monitoring and appropriate regulatory responses.

provisions.title: COVID-19 Financial Stability Response
provisions.text: At the Riyadh Summit in 2020, G20 leaders endorsed the financial stability response to the COVID-19 pandemic. Key measures included: use of flexibility within existing regulatory frameworks (allowing banks to use capital and liquidity buffers); supervisory measures to support lending (relief on expected credit loss provisioning); temporary adjustments to market risk requirements; and extension of implementation timelines for Basel III reforms. Leaders tasked the FSB with assessing lessons learned from the pandemic response and identifying areas where the regulatory framework needed to be strengthened.

provisions.title: Sustainable Finance Roadmap
provisions.text: At the Rome Summit in 2021, G20 leaders endorsed the G20 Sustainable Finance Roadmap. The roadmap covers five focus areas: improving the consistency of sustainability reporting and disclosure; identifying sustainable finance instruments and their alignment with climate and sustainability goals; strengthening the role of international financial institutions in supporting the transition; developing the role of technology and innovation in sustainable finance; and overcoming barriers to sustainable finance in developing countries. The roadmap was updated in 2022 to include a transition finance framework.

provisions.title: Financial Inclusion and Digital Public Infrastructure
provisions.text: The G20 under Brazil's presidency (2024) focused on financial inclusion through digital public infrastructure. Key themes included: promoting interoperability between instant payment systems across borders; expanding access to digital financial services; ensuring responsible use of AI in financial services; and addressing risks from tokenisation and central bank digital currencies (CBDCs). Leaders endorsed principles for responsible innovation in digital finance.

provisions.title: G20 Principles for CBDC Design
provisions.text: The G20 has endorsed BIS and FSB work on central bank digital currencies (CBDCs). Key design principles include: CBDCs should complement (not replace) existing forms of money; CBDCs should support monetary and financial stability; CBDC design should minimise risks to the financial system (bank disintermediation, run risk); cross-border CBDC arrangements should be interoperable; and CBDC design should address AML/CFT requirements, data privacy, and cyber security. The BIS Innovation Hub is conducting CBDC experimentation projects (mBridge, Icebreaker) to test cross-border CBDC arrangements.

provisions.title: Common Framework for Debt Treatment
provisions.text: The G20 established the Common Framework for Debt Treatment beyond the Debt Service Suspension Initiative (DSSI) to address sovereign debt vulnerabilities in low-income countries. The framework provides a mechanism for coordinated debt treatment involving both Paris Club and non-Paris Club official bilateral creditors. Key principles include: comparability of treatment across creditors; adequate information sharing; and private sector participation on comparable terms. The framework has been applied to cases including Chad, Ethiopia, and Zambia.

provisions.title: CCP Recovery: Loss Allocation Tools
provisions.text: CPMI-IOSCO has published guidance on recovery of CCPs. Recovery tools include: variation margin gains haircutting (VMGH) to allocate losses to participants; cash calls (additional contributions to the default fund); partial tear-up of contracts (involuntary closing out of positions); service closure (ceasing to provide clearing services for specific products); and initial margin haircutting. CCPs should have a comprehensive recovery plan that specifies the tools available, the conditions under which they may be used, and the governance procedures for their activation.

provisions.title: CCP Resolution: Authorities and Tools
provisions.text: The FSB has developed guidance on CCP resolution, building on the Key Attributes. Resolution authorities should have powers to: continue critical CCP functions; allocate losses to CCP participants and equity holders; transfer positions and assets to a bridge CCP or a solvent CCP; impose a moratorium on the exercise of termination rights; and require additional financial resources from CCP participants. The resolution strategy should preserve the continuity of the CCP's critical functions (clearing services) and should only be triggered when recovery measures have failed or are unlikely to succeed.

provisions.title: Guidance on Cyber Resilience for Financial Market Infrastructures
provisions.text: CPMI-IOSCO has published guidance on cyber resilience for FMIs. The guidance supplements Principle 17 (Operational Risk) of the PFMI. Key areas include: governance (board-level oversight of cyber risk); identification (asset management, risk assessment, threat intelligence); protection (access control, data security, protective technology); detection (continuous monitoring, anomaly detection, penetration testing); response (incident response planning, communication, analysis); and recovery (recovery planning, testing). FMIs should assume that cyber attacks will occur and should be able to resume critical operations within two hours of a disruptive event (2-hour recovery time objective).

provisions.title: Application of PFMI to Stablecoin Arrangements
provisions.text: CPMI-IOSCO has clarified that the Principles for Financial Market Infrastructures apply to systemically important stablecoin arrangements that perform FMI functions (transfer, clearing, settlement). Stablecoin arrangements must meet the same standards as traditional FMIs including: legal basis (Principle 1); governance (Principle 2); risk management (Principle 3); settlement finality (Principle 8); money settlements (Principle 9); and operational risk (Principle 17). Stablecoin arrangements that include a transfer function are expected to meet the PFMI regardless of the technology used.

provisions.title: CCP Margin Model Validation and Review
provisions.text: CCPs should regularly review and validate their margin models. Validation should include: conceptual soundness review; ongoing monitoring (backtesting, sensitivity analysis, stress testing); independent model review; and benchmarking against alternative methodologies. Margin models should be backtested at least monthly, comparing predicted margin coverage against actual portfolio losses. CCPs should maintain a margin model validation framework that includes governance arrangements, escalation procedures, and regular reporting to the board.

provisions.title: Harmonisation of OTC Derivatives Data Elements
provisions.text: CPMI-IOSCO has developed a set of harmonised data elements for OTC derivatives reporting to trade repositories. The harmonised data elements cover: counterparty data (LEI, counterparty role); product data (UPI, classification); transaction data (notional, price, settlement); event data (modifications, terminations); and valuation data (mark-to-market, collateral). The harmonisation project uses the ISO 20022 standard and the Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) as key data standards. Implementation timelines vary by jurisdiction.

provisions.title: Fintech and Deposit Insurance
provisions.text: IADI has published research on the implications of fintech for deposit insurance systems. Key areas include: deposit-taking by neobanks and digital-only banks (applying consistent membership and coverage requirements); e-money and digital wallets (whether balances constitute insured deposits); open banking and account aggregation (implications for SCV data and payout processes); DeFi and stablecoins (whether stablecoin reserves held at banks create insured deposits); and real-time payments (implications for deposit flows and run dynamics). Deposit insurers should monitor fintech developments and adapt their frameworks accordingly.

provisions.title: Deposit Insurance Payout Practices
provisions.text: IADI has published guidance on deposit insurance payout practices. Best practices include: maintaining up-to-date depositor information through single customer view (SCV) requirements; pre-positioning payout systems and testing them regularly; providing multiple payout channels (bank transfer, cheque, digital payment); establishing temporary high-balance coverage for specific events (house purchase, insurance payout); communicating clearly with depositors during a payout event; and coordinating with the resolution authority on the timing and method of payout. The target payout timeframe is within seven working days.

provisions.title: Deposit Insurance Fund Management
provisions.text: IADI has published guidance on deposit insurance fund management. Key considerations include: fund target ratio (typically 1-5% of covered deposits, varying by jurisdiction); funding method (ex ante premiums vs ex post levies, or hybrid); risk-based premium assessment (premiums differentiated based on bank risk profile); fund investment policies (safety, liquidity, diversification); back-up funding arrangements (government credit facilities for extraordinary events); and fund adequacy assessment (stress testing the fund under various failure scenarios).
